evidence: `examples/common/bootstrap.py` imports credential/url resolvers from `thenvoi.testing.config_loader`, so example runtime wiring depends on a testing namespace (`examples/common/bootstrap.py:11`, `src/thenvoi/testing/config_loader.py:35-82`).

`tests/support/integration/config.py` combines pytest plugin hooks (`pytest_addoption`, `pytest_runtest_setup`) with API fixture provisioning and cleanup orchestration in one highly imported module (`tests/support/integration/config.py:87-190`, `335-711`).

Thenvoi link test doubles are implemented in multiple places with overlapping but different contracts (`tests/support/fakes_link.py:24-89`, `tests/bridge/conftest.py:23-58`, `tests/platform/test_link_contacts.py:31-231`).

Tool execution abstraction is split across multiple paths: `AnthropicAdapter` calls `tools.execute_tool_call(...)` while `PydanticAIAdapter`, `CrewAIAdapter`, and `ClaudeSDKAdapter` bypass that protocol and call `invoke_platform_tool(...)` with framework-local wrappers.

Room/session state lifecycle is repeated per adapter (`_message_history`, `_room_tools`, `_session_context`, `_room_sessions`, `_room_customers`, bootstrap flags) even though `SimpleAdapter` already contains partial staging helpers.

The adapter callback contract carries a broad parameter bag (`msg`, `tools`, `history`, `participants_msg`, `contacts_msg`, `is_session_bootstrap`, `room_id`), and some adapters (for example A2A gateway) receive fields they do not use, indicating interface overbreadth.

Compatibility indirection remains active in runtime paths: deprecated symbol forwarding in `compat/shims.py`, passthrough aliases in `PlatformRuntime` (`_queue_broadcast`, `_inject_hub_event`, `_inject_hub_system_prompt`), and tests still importing deprecated paths.

`src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a_bridge/handler.py` defines a public contract (`HandlerResult` status validation, factory constructors, and `BaseHandler.handle` return semantics) but coverage is mostly indirect through `tests/bridge/test_router.py` behavior tests.

No tests reference the legacy normalization sentinel path (`legacy_implicit_return`) used by dispatcher compatibility handling, despite `BaseHandler.handle` explicitly allowing `None` for backward compatibility.

The only direct constructor guard test in `tests/bridge/test_router.py` checks invalid status rejection; there is no focused test module that locks down `handled()/ignored()/error()` default semantics as API contract.
`ExecutionContext` combines queue orchestration, retry policy, sync/recovery, participant cache, context hydration, and message lifecycle handling in one class (`src/thenvoi/runtime/execution.py`).

`CodexAdapter` mixes transport lifecycle, command parsing, approval UX/state, sandbox normalization, model fallback, task-event formatting, and tool dispatch in a single 1955-line adapter (`src/thenvoi/adapters/codex.py`).

Tool wrapping/formatting/session access responsibilities are implemented independently across framework modules (`src/thenvoi/adapters/claude_sdk.py`, `src/thenvoi/integrations/claude_sdk/tools.py`, `src/thenvoi/integrations/parlant/tools.py`) despite a shared bridge existing in `src/thenvoi/runtime/tool_bridge.py`.

findings.summary: Example runtime bootstrap crosses into the testing package, blurring production-vs-test module boundaries.
evidence: `bootstrap.py` imports `resolve_agent_credentials` and `resolve_platform_urls` from `thenvoi.testing.config_loader` (`examples/common/bootstrap.py:11`).
Example scripts consume `bootstrap_agent()` as their runtime entrypoint (`examples/a2a_bridge/02_with_auth.py:25,58`), so examples inherit testing-layer dependency direction.

`thenvoi.testing.config_loader` includes compatibility aliases and runner-specific loading concerns (`load_runner_config`, `load_config`) that are not example-runtime specific (`src/thenvoi/testing/config_loader.py:62-82`).
findings.suggestion: Extract environment/credential resolution into a neutral runtime config module under non-testing package boundaries (for example `thenvoi.config`), then have `thenvoi.testing.config_loader` and `examples/common/bootstrap.py` both depend on that shared core.

findings.summary: Bridge/platform tests maintain parallel ThenvoiLink mock surfaces instead of one shared contract boundary.
evidence: `tests/support/fakes_link.py` defines a canonical `make_thenvoi_link_mock()` with lifecycle/subscription/rest behavior (`24-89`).
`tests/bridge/conftest.py` rebuilds separate MagicMock link objects manually with partially overlapping methods (`23-58`) instead of reusing the shared fake.
`tests/platform/test_link_contacts.py` bypasses the shared fake and patches `WebSocketClient` directly in each test (`31-231`), creating a third pattern for the same boundary.
findings.suggestion: Define a single shared test-double boundary for `ThenvoiLink`/socket behavior (fixture factory + typed helper API), and migrate bridge/platform tests to consume it so contract changes are centralized.

findings.summary: Integration support module is a hub that mixes plugin policy, live cleanup orchestration, settings cache, and fixtures, increasing cross-module coupling.
evidence: `tests/support/integration/config.py` contains pytest plugin hooks (`pytest_addoption`, `pytest_runtest_setup`) and fixture implementations in the same module (`87-190`, `540-711`).
The same module also owns a module-global mutable settings singleton (`_test_settings`) used across fixtures (`489-497`, `540-600`), introducing shared state coupling.

Integration tests consume marker/decorator layer (`tests/support/integration/markers.py`, `tests/integration/test_smoke.py:8-14`) while fixture enforcement policy lives elsewhere, splitting policy/runtime concerns across tightly coupled support modules.
findings.suggestion: Split integration architecture into explicit boundaries: a dedicated pytest plugin module for marker policy and command-line options, separate stateless fixture modules for clients/resources, and fixture-scoped settings objects instead of module-global cache.

findings.summary: Adapters use multiple incompatible tool-dispatch abstractions, duplicating glue logic and weakening the common tools interface.
evidence: `AnthropicAdapter` executes platform tools via `tools.execute_tool_call(tool_name, tool_input)`.
`PydanticAIAdapter` and `CrewAIAdapter` call `invoke_platform_tool(...)` directly and build framework-specific wrappers/signatures via `TOOL_BINDINGS`.
`ClaudeSDKAdapter` also routes through `invoke_platform_tool(...)` inside MCP handlers, bypassing the `ToolDispatchProtocol` path.
`AgentTools.execute_tool_call` still performs its own validation/dispatch path, so tool call policy and error-shaping are spread across adapter-local code and runtime tools.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one dispatch contract. Prefer a single runtime-level `execute_tool_call` API with shared validation/error mapping and have all adapters call that surface (including MCP/SDK integrations), while `TOOL_BINDINGS` only describes schema shape.

findings.summary: Per-room/session state handling is reimplemented across adapters instead of being centralized, increasing lifecycle drift risk.
evidence: SimpleAdapter provides `stage_room_state`, `stage_room_history`, and cleanup helpers, but adapters still manage multiple custom room registries (`_message_history`, `_room_tools`, `_session_context`, `_session_ids`, `_room_sessions`, `_room_customers`, `_bootstrapped_rooms`).
Claude SDK stores multiple correlated dictionaries keyed by room (`_room_tools`, `_session_context`, `_session_ids`) with bespoke init/cleanup behavior.
Parlant tracks both session and customer mappings per room with adapter-specific lifecycle logic.
LangGraph carries separate bootstrap tracking set to prevent duplicate prompt injection, another room-lifecycle variant.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared room-session state manager abstraction (typed state container + bootstrap/cleanup policy hooks) and migrate adapters to register state schemas instead of each adapter hand-rolling room dictionaries.

findings.summary: The adapter `on_message` signature acts as a parameter bag, forcing all adapters to accept cross-cutting fields even when not relevant.
evidence: `SimpleAdapter.on_message` requires seven message-context arguments/flags (`msg`, `tools`, `history`, `participants_msg`, `contacts_msg`, `is_session_bootstrap`, `room_id`).
A2A gateway adapter receives this full signature but ignores participant/contact broadcast fields in its control-plane flow.
Tests construct full `AgentInput` payloads with many optional cross-cutting fields, confirming a broad generalized contract even for simpler handlers.
findings.suggestion: Replace the multi-parameter callback with a typed context object (for example `MessageTurnContext`) exposing optional facets, and split separate adapter base contracts for room-bound chat adapters vs control-plane adapters.

findings.summary: Compatibility wrappers remain in active runtime/test paths, adding pass-through indirection without clear enforcement of sunset.
evidence: A large forwarding registry in `compat/shims.py` still maps old module symbols (including runtime contact and bridge surfaces) through dynamic `__getattr__` forwarding.
`PlatformRuntime` keeps private alias wrappers that only forward (`_queue_broadcast`, `_inject_hub_event`, `_inject_hub_system_prompt`) alongside canonical methods.
Tests still import deprecated path `thenvoi.runtime.contact_tools`, demonstrating compatibility indirection remains a first-class dependency, not just legacy edge support.
findings.suggestion: Create and execute a compatibility retirement plan: track shim call sites, migrate tests/importers to canonical modules, add CI guardrails for new deprecated-path imports, and remove passthrough aliases once coverage confirms no active callers.

findings.summary: Bridge handler contract is tested mostly via router flow, leaving core API invariants under-specified at unit level.
evidence: `handler.py` exposes `HandlerResult` as a standalone API with validation and convenience constructors, but tests are embedded in router-focused scenarios rather than dedicated contract tests.
`tests/bridge/test_router.py` exercises routing and lifecycle marking heavily; it does not comprehensively assert `HandlerResult` constructor/factory defaults as a stable API surface.
This creates coupling where handler contract regressions can be masked by unrelated router behavior changes.
findings.suggestion: Add a focused test module (for example `tests/bridge/test_handler_contract.py`) that directly validates `HandlerResult` statuses, default/detail behavior, and constructor/factory parity independent of routing side effects.

findings.summary: Backward-compatible `None` return semantics declared in handler contract are not explicitly tested, despite being a critical dispatch compatibility path.
evidence: `BaseHandler.handle` return type in `handler.py` allows `HandlerResult | None` and documents `None` as backward-compatible implicit success.
`route_dispatch.py` contains explicit normalization logic for this (`if result is None: return HandlerResult.handled()` and fallback detail `legacy_implicit_return`).
Current bridge tests do not assert the `None`-return normalization behavior or the `legacy_implicit_return` compatibility branch.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit test with a handler returning `None` and assert dispatch marks processed (not failed), plus a test for non-`HandlerResult` legacy return normalization to prevent silent compatibility regressions.

findings.summary: Example runtime bootstrap depends on `thenvoi.testing` internals instead of a stable config boundary.
evidence: `examples/common/bootstrap.py` imports `resolve_agent_credentials` and `resolve_platform_urls` from `thenvoi.testing.config_loader` for normal example startup.
`README.md` positions examples as standard usage entrypoints, but this path relies on a namespace semantically tied to testing support.
No explicit compatibility contract is declared in `pyproject.toml` for `thenvoi.testing` as a public/stable example-facing API surface.
findings.suggestion: Promote credential/url resolution to a non-testing public module (for example `thenvoi.config`), update examples to consume that API, and keep `thenvoi.testing.config_loader` as a deprecation shim with a documented sunset.

findings.summary: Documented CI contract gates are not represented as enforceable repository-level test selection rules.
evidence: `README.md` claims PR-required contract checks (bridge/bootstrap/prompt/integration marker policy).
`pyproject.toml` only sets default pytest `addopts = "-v -m 'not integration'"` and marker registration; it does not encode a contract-check test suite selector or policy gate.
Given this split, governance depends on external CI wiring rather than an in-repo executable contract, increasing drift risk between docs and runtime checks.
findings.suggestion: Add explicit pytest marker groups or dedicated commands in `pyproject.toml` (or documented task runner targets) for each required contract gate, and reference those exact commands in `README.md` so policy is reproducible locally and in CI.

findings.summary: Integration fixture docs include commands that conflict with default pytest marker filtering.
evidence: `tests/support/integration/config.py` docstring repeatedly suggests `uv run pytest tests/integration/ -v` without `-m integration`.
`pyproject.toml` globally applies `-m 'not integration'`, so that command path can exclude integration tests by default.
`README.md` uses `-m integration` in integration sections, creating two competing run contracts inside the same codebase.
findings.suggestion: Normalize all integration run instructions to include `-m integration` (or update `addopts` strategy), and add a single authoritative command reference reused by both README and integration support module docs.

findings.summary: ExecutionContext conflates multiple runtime responsibilities and duplicates message lifecycle logic.
evidence: `_process_backlog_message` and `_process_event` both implement near-identical lifecycle steps (mark_processing -> hydrate -> execute -> mark_processed/mark_failed), with separate branching and error paths.
`ExecutionContext` also owns participant cache mutation, context hydration, dedupe/retry tracking, stale message recovery, and queue synchronization in the same class, increasing change blast radius.
`PlatformRuntime` directly injects broadcast/system events into execution internals, reinforcing tight coupling to one oversized execution implementation.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `MessageLifecycleProcessor` used by both backlog and websocket paths, and split `ExecutionContext` into focused collaborators (sync/recovery, participant/context cache, queue executor). Keep `PlatformRuntime` talking to a narrow execution interface instead of execution internals.

findings.summary: Large adapters bundle unrelated concerns (transport, policy, UX commands, and event formatting).
evidence: `CodexAdapter` contains local slash-command UX (`_handle_local_command`), manual approval queueing (`_resolve_manual_approval`), sandbox normalization, model fallback, RPC request handling, and event formatting in one class.
`CrewAIAdapter` combines global event-loop patching (`nest_asyncio`), dynamic tool class generation, execution-reporting policy, and chat history/session orchestration in one adapter module.
`ClaudeSDKAdapter` builds MCP server/tool wrappers inline (`_create_mcp_server`) while also handling session management and message processing.
findings.suggestion: Refactor adapters into composition-based components: session/transport client, turn/event processor, approval-policy service, and tool-binding factory. Keep adapter classes as thin framework facades wiring these components together.

findings.summary: Framework-specific tool wrappers re-implement bridge/session behavior, causing design drift.
evidence: `tool_bridge.py` centralizes normalization/dispatch/error contracts, but `ClaudeSDKAdapter._create_mcp_server` independently defines result/error formatting and room-tool lookup closures.
`integrations/claude_sdk/tools.py` duplicates similar MCP wrapper concerns (`_make_result`, `_make_error`, `_get_tools`) and custom descriptor mapping logic.
`integrations/parlant/tools.py` uses module-level session registries (`_session_tools`, `_session_message_sent`) and custom formatting flows, creating another tool-execution path shape.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared framework-agnostic `ToolBindingFactory` + `ToolSessionRegistry` abstraction and make Claude/Parlant wrappers consume it. Keep only framework-specific decorator syntax in integration modules.

findings.summary: Numbered gateway examples are thin wrappers over separate implementation files, inviting drift.
evidence: `02_with_demo_agent.py` contains long usage/docs but delegates execution to `with_demo_agent.main`, while the real runtime logic lives elsewhere.
`01_basic_gateway.py` follows the same wrapper pattern (delegating to another module), so explanatory docs and executable behavior can diverge over time.
This creates duplicate entrypoint conventions (`NN_*.py` wrappers + non-numbered implementations) for one example family.
findings.suggestion: Use a single canonical executable file per example and keep numbered variants as minimal aliases generated from that source (or remove aliases). Store documentation in one shared location to avoid wrapper-doc drift.
